metadata.title: Why cutting a 402-line agent prompt in half — while keeping every fact — makes it faster and better
metadata.topic: Decomposing a monolith prompt into tools, skills, and code execution
metadata.purpose: Tools, skills, and subagents trade cost for control; a monolith forces all knowledge into one window, while decomposition lets the agent load only what a task needs, keeping context lean and decisions focused.
metadata.clock: narration (Kokoro am_onyx) — master clock; conform each beat to measured audio
metadata.exclusions: the full 12-tool API; eval-suite mechanics; how to author a skill
metadata.visual_object: A decomposition tree — 402-line prompt splitting into a 15-line core + skill modules + tools, with latency/cost deltas at each branch.
metadata.hook: A 402-line system prompt with 12 tools is slow and expensive; a 15-line prompt holding the same knowledge in skills runs faster and answers better.

beats.narration_text: A daily low-stock sweep made 102 tool calls over 488 seconds; after splitting knowledge into on-demand skills and switching to code execution, the same task runs as 3 bash scripts in ~100 seconds — same correctness, far fewer tokens.
beats.scaffold_note: from Key case — cold open on the concrete instance

beats.narration_text: Why does halving the prompt while keeping all the knowledge improve reasoning instead of degrading it?
A 402-line system prompt with 12 tools is slow and expensive; a 15-line prompt holding the same knowledge in skills runs faster and answers better.

Tools, skills, and subagents trade cost for control; a monolith forces all knowledge into one window, while decomposition lets the agent load only what a task needs, keeping context lean and decisions focused.
beats.visual: A decomposition tree — 402-line prompt splitting into a 15-line core + skill modules + tools, with latency/cost deltas at each branch.
beats.scaffold_note: the resolving mechanism; orbits the visual object

beats.narration_text: A 200-line reorder-policy skill is uploaded separately; the core shrinks to "use reorder-policy skill to decide stock levels," loaded only on reorder tasks.

Tools, skills, and subagents trade cost for control; a monolith forces all knowledge into one window, while decomposition lets the agent load only what a task needs, keeping context lean and decisions focused.
